1: The beginning of Jehoiakim's reign — the same king who burned Jeremiah's scroll (Jer 36). The dating links the temple-sermon trial narrative to a regime of active hostility to the prophetic word. Jesus operates under the same structural hostility: Luke 13:31 ("leave this place, because Herod wants to kill you") and John 11:53 ("from that day on, they plotted to take his life"). Both prophets deliver their most dangerous messages knowing the political climate has already decided against them.
2: "Stand in the court of the LORD's temple and speak to all the people from the cities of Judah — do not omit a single word." The command to full disclosure — no softening, no omitting. Jesus teaches in the temple courts daily (Luke 19:47; Jeremiah 21:37) and likewise declares "I have spoken openly to the world" (John 18:20 — "I always taught in synagogues or at the temple, where all the Jews come together. I said nothing in secret"). The prophet commanded to hide nothing in the temple foreshadows the prophet-king who taught the full truth publicly from the same courts.
3: "Perhaps they will listen and turn from their evil way. Then I can relent." The divine "perhaps" — the genuine openness of divine repentance-response. 2 Peter 3:9 — "the Lord is not slow in keeping his promise... he is patient with you, not wanting anyone to perish, but everyone to come to repentance." Christ's weeping over Jerusalem (Luke 19:41-44) is the incarnate form of the divine "perhaps": the open possibility of repentance that was not taken. "How often I have longed to gather your children together... but you were not willing" (Matthew 23:37) — the "perhaps" of Jeremiah 26:3 came to personal expression in Christ's grief.
4: "If you will not listen to me, if you will not walk in my law." The conditional structure of the warning — the outcome depends on response. Matthew 23:37-38 — "O Jerusalem, Jerusalem... look, your house is left to you desolate." Jesus's final temple address is the conditional of Jeremiah 26:4-6 brought to its term: the condition was not met, the desolation follows. His pronouncement is not vindictive but the logical consequence of the covenant structure Jeremiah articulates here.
5: "If you will not listen to the words of my servants the prophets whom I keep sending to you urgently." The persistent-sending motif again (hashkem) — the long sequence of divine patience. Matthew 21:33-38 — the parable of the tenants who beat and killed the servants, then killed the son. Jesus identifies himself as the "last messenger" in a sequence of rejected prophets (Matthew 23:35-37). The servants sent urgently in Jeremiah 26:5 are the slaves beaten and killed in the parable; the son sent last is Christ himself.
6: "I will make this house like Shiloh, and I will make this city a curse for all the nations on earth." Shiloh — where the tabernacle stood before the Philistines captured the ark (1 Sam 4) — is the prototype of divine presence withdrawn from a sanctuary. Jesus makes the same prophecy about the temple: Matthew 24:2 — "not one stone here will be left on another; every one will be thrown down." John 2:19 — "destroy this temple, and I will raise it in three days" — he redirects the Shiloh-prophecy to his own body, the true temple that is destroyed and rebuilt. The house-like-Shiloh curse of Jeremiah 26:6 is absorbed and transformed in Christ's resurrection.
7: The priests, prophets, and all the people heard Jeremiah's words — the full religious establishment witnesses the sermon. The parallel at Jesus's temple-cleansing: Matthew 21:15 — "the chief priests and the teachers of the law saw the wonderful things he did and the children shouting... and they were indignant." In both cases the full establishment is present; in both cases they respond with indignation and a plan for violence rather than repentance. The witness of the institutions is the witness that condemns itself.
8: "The priests, the prophets, and all the people seized him, saying: You must die!" The mob arrest. Matthew 26:47-50 — "while he was still speaking, Judas came, accompanied by a large crowd armed with swords and clubs." Mark 14:46 — "the men seized Jesus and arrested him." The "you must die" verdict of the mob precedes and mirrors the "he is worthy of death" verdict of the council (Matthew 26:66). Both Jeremiah and Jesus are seized by a religious mob after prophetic speech in/about the temple. The structural parallel is unmistakable.
9: "Why have you prophesied in the name of the LORD, saying that this temple will become like Shiloh?" The charge: prophesying against the temple. Matthew 26:61 — "this fellow said, 'I am able to destroy the temple of God and rebuild it in three days.'" Mark 14:58 — "we heard him say, 'I will destroy this man-made temple and in three days will build another, not made by human hands.'" The false witnesses at Jesus's trial repurpose his temple-prophecy as the charge against him, exactly as Jeremiah's temple-sermon is the charge against him. The prophecy about the temple becomes the grounds for the prophet's death.
10: The officials of Judah come up from the palace to the temple — the political establishment joins the religious mob. At Jesus's trial, the same combination: the chief priests and elders (religious establishment) combined with Pilate and the Roman officials (political establishment). Both trials involve the intersection of religious and political authority conspiring against the prophet. Acts 4:27 — "both Herod and Pontius Pilate met together with the Gentiles and the people of Israel in this city to conspire against your holy servant Jesus."
11: "This man deserves to die — he has prophesied against this city." The priests' and prophets' verdict: mišpaṭ māwet — a sentence of death. Matthew 26:66 — "what do you think? He is worthy of death (enochos thanatou)." The Sanhedrin's verdict on Jesus is the exact equivalent of the priests' verdict on Jeremiah: the deserving-of-death charge for prophesying against the holy city. The accusation structure, the tribunal, and the verdict are parallel — Jeremiah's trial is the type; Jesus's trial is the antitype.
12: "The LORD sent me to prophesy against this house and this city all the words you have heard." Jeremiah's defense: divine commission. Jesus before the Sanhedrin: John 18:20-21 — "I said nothing in secret. Why question me? Ask those who heard me." Before Pilate: John 18:36-37 — "the reason I was born and came into the world is to testify to the truth." Both prophets ground their defense not in self-interest but in the truth of their divine commission. The messenger is not on trial for being a messenger; the message itself is what is at stake.
13: "Reform your ways and your deeds and obey the voice of the LORD your God, and the LORD will relent." The invitation to repentance embedded within the defense — Jeremiah does not merely seek acquittal but genuinely desires the repentance that would make his prophecies void. Acts 2:38 — Peter, in the shadow of the recently crucified Christ, issues the same invitation: "repent and be baptized." Acts 3:19 — "repent and turn to God, so that your sins may be wiped out, that times of refreshing may come from the Lord." The prophet who desires repentance rather than destruction is Christ incarnate.
14: "As for me, I am in your hands. Do with me whatever you consider good and right." Jeremiah's surrender to the verdict. Isaiah 53:7 — "he was oppressed and afflicted, yet he did not open his mouth; he was led like a lamb to the slaughter." John 18:11 — "Shall I not drink the cup the Father has given me?" John 19:11 — "you would have no power over me if it were not given to you from above." Jeremiah's "I am in your hands" is the proto-passion moment — the prophet who submits himself to human judgment as the ultimate act of prophetic integrity. Christ's submission is identical in form and vastly deeper in content: he is actually innocent, and his submission is the atonement Jeremiah's only foreshadows.
15: "But know this for certain: if you put me to death, you will bring the guilt of innocent blood upon yourselves, upon this city, and upon its inhabitants." Innocent blood — dam-nāqî. Matthew 27:4 — Judas: "I have sinned, for I have betrayed innocent blood (haima athōon)." Matthew 27:24 — Pilate: "I am innocent of this man's blood (tou haimatos tou dikaiou toutou — the blood of this righteous man)." The "innocent blood" terminology of Jeremiah 26:15 is used verbatim in the Passion narrative to describe the death of Christ — the evangelists are making a deliberate parallel to Jeremiah's trial. Christ's death brings the "innocent blood" guilt upon the city that Jeremiah warned about, and yet through his prayer (Luke 23:34 — "Father, forgive them") he intercedes against the very guilt he warned of.
16: "This man does not deserve death — he has spoken to us in the name of the LORD our God." The officials' acquittal of Jeremiah. The partial parallel to Pilate: John 18:38; Jeremiah 19:4, 6 — "I find no basis for a charge against him" (three times Pilate declares Jesus innocent). But the acquittals diverge in outcome: Jeremiah is released; Jesus is crucified despite Pilate's three-fold declaration of innocence. The acquittal that saves Jeremiah becomes the acquittal that the crowd overrides in Christ's case — his innocence is declared and then overridden by the mob's "crucify him!"
17: The elders of the land who stand to give the precedent speech — the appeal to tradition in defense of prophetic speech. James 5:10 — "brothers and sisters, as an example of patience in the face of suffering, take the prophets who spoke in the name of the Lord." The elders who invoke prophetic precedent to protect Jeremiah are doing what the NT writers do: reading the prophets' suffering as the pattern that Christ fulfills and that his followers share. The precedent-speech of Jeremiah 26:17 is the hermeneutical practice of reading prophetic history christologically.
18: The elders cite Micah 3:12 — "Zion will be plowed like a field, Jerusalem will become a heap of rubble." The use of one prophet to defend another — intertextual solidarity. Jesus invokes the same prophetic solidarity: Matthew 5:12 — "in the same way they persecuted the prophets who were before you." Luke 24:44-45 — "everything must be fulfilled that is written about me in the Law of Moses, the Prophets, and the Psalms." The prophets quote each other, fulfill each other, and ultimately all point to Christ as the prophet who summates and surpasses them all.
19: "Did Hezekiah put Micah to death? No — he feared the LORD and the LORD relented." Hezekiah as the positive precedent — the king who responds to prophetic warning with repentance. The contrast with Jehoiakim (who burns the scroll) and the contrast with those who killed Jesus is sharp. Hezekiah's fear of the LORD and seeking of divine favor (2 Kings 19:14-19) is the royal model that points toward Christ's own Gethsemane prayer — "not my will but yours" — as the ultimate example of reverent submission (Hebrews 5:7 — "he was heard because of his reverent submission").
20: Urijah son of Shemaiah — another prophet who prophesied the same message as Jeremiah against Jerusalem and the temple. The existence of multiple prophets with the same message is the interlinear confirmation of divine intention: two or three witnesses establish the word (Deuteronomy 19:15; Matthew 18:16). Jesus himself notes the pattern: Matthew 23:37 — "Jerusalem, Jerusalem, you who kill the prophets and stone those sent to you." Urijah is one more in this sequence of sent-and-killed prophets that runs from Abel (Matthew 23:35) to Christ himself.
21: King Jehoiakim sought to have Urijah executed — and Urijah fled to Egypt. The prophet who fled versus the prophet who stayed. Jeremiah stays; Christ stays in Jerusalem when he knows it means his death (Luke 13:33 — "no prophet can die outside Jerusalem!" — Jesus's self-conscious direction toward the city of his death). The contrast between Urijah's flight to Egypt and Jeremiah's remaining is the contrast Christ intensifies: he is the prophet who set his face like flint (Isaiah 50:7) and did not flee.
22: Elnathan son of Achbor sent to Egypt to extradite Urijah — the king's long arm extending to Egypt. The irony is that Jeremiah would later be dragged to Egypt against his will (Jer 43), and Urijah is dragged from Egypt to Jerusalem. The prophets are controlled neither by geography nor by kings but by YHWH's purposes. Christ's own birth narrative included an Egypt-flight (Matthew 2:14-15 — "that it might be fulfilled: out of Egypt I called my son") that inverts Urijah's extradition and repeats the Exodus-pattern.
23: "They brought Urijah back from Egypt to King Jehoiakim, who had him executed. His body was thrown into the common burial ground." The prophet killed and dishonorably buried — the death of a servant cast aside. This is what they did to Christ in intention: the mockery, the crucifixion, the borrowed tomb. But the common burial of Urijah contrasts sharply with Isaiah 53:9 ("his grave was assigned with the wicked") and its reversal ("and with the rich in his death" — he was buried in Joseph of Arimathea's new tomb). The prophet thrown into the common grave anticipates and contrasts with the prophet-king buried in a garden tomb and raised on the third day.
24: "But Ahikam son of Shaphan used his influence to protect Jeremiah, so that he was not handed over to the people to be executed." The hidden protector — the powerful official who uses his position to shelter the prophet. Joseph of Arimathea plays an identical role for Jesus: Mark 15:43-46 — "a prominent member of the council, who was himself waiting for the kingdom of God, went boldly to Pilate and asked for Jesus' body... Joseph bought some linen cloth, took down the body, wrapped it." Both Ahikam and Joseph are described as officials who use their influence for the dead/endangered prophet against the expected outcome of mob justice.
